summary: The post argues that confusing data job titles create unstable pay bands and inequitable compensation across the industry. Stancil contends that analysts’ work—translating messy business problems into persuasive, high‑impact decisions—is harder to develop and more valuable than analytics engineering, yet Silicon Valley’s bias toward technical skills inflates engineer pay. He calls for intentionally paying analysts more to counter this bias and to elevate “soft” skills often gendered and undervalued.
money_quotes: If we don’t talk about pay—because it’s complicated, because it’s taboo, or because we assume it’ll just sort itself out—pay bands and industry habits will settle along the same biased lines that they always have.

These sets of responsibilities require different skills, some of which are much harder to acquire and hone than others. We should pay people accordingly—and, therefore, analysts should make more than analytics engineers.
It’s telling, I think, that this feels like a radical proposal.
To me, the most plausible explanation is a simpler one: Silicon Valley overvalues technical skills.
By paying analysts more, we can also break down some of the historic rot that has denigrated “feminine” soft skills.
key_insight: The industry’s default bias toward technical work distorts pay, and correcting it means intentionally valuing analysts’ interpersonal, problem-framing, and influence skills more highly.
